business_info: The effective grade is the average between the high and low grade.

School code formatting in the source systems is not fully standardized; legacy exports may include leading or trailing whitespace, embedded hyphens, or district-specific prefixes. Downstream consumers are expected to normalize codes before performing joins or comparisons.

For internal analytics, "school code" may refer either to the CDS code or to a locally assigned campus ID, depending on the report owner. Unless explicitly stated, analysts should not assume one-to-one alignment between these identifiers across all tables.

Historically, some charter campuses have reported enrollment figures based on October census day while district-managed schools used end-of-year headcounts; this discrepancy is not backfilled, so analysts comparing enrollment across campuses should document which convention is being used.

In some older data pulls, the total enrollment field may exclude independently funded special programs (e.g., continuation schools, adult ed). Where this occurs, the missing headcount is not labeled or adjusted and must be inferred from external rosters if precise totals are needed.

District accountability reports sometimes reference a "weighted enrollment" metric that adjusts total headcount for partial-year attendees; this metric is NOT stored in the core schools table and should not be conflated with the regular enrollment column.

The term "effective grade span" is occasionally used in planning documents to describe the range of grades a school is projected to serve over the next three years and may not match the historical grade span encoded in the data warehouse.

Because grade spans for K–8 and 6–12 schools are sometimes reconfigured mid-year, some warehouses keep both "operational grade span" and "chartering grade span" fields; analysts should check with the data governance group before using these for funding or accountability calculations.

California uses multiple standardized tests over time (STAR, CAHSEE, SBAC, SAT, ACT). In legacy dashboards, the phrase "math proficiency" may refer to very different underlying exams and scales; SAT math is only one of several possible math-related indicators.

In internal reporting, the label "college readiness score" is sometimes used as a catch-all for SAT composite, ACT composite, or an internal index combining GPA and course-taking patterns; always confirm which underlying metric is referenced before aggregating across schools.

Many SAT-related extracts only include scores for students who opted in to send results to the district; these files will undercount total test takers and may skew average scores upward relative to state-published statistics.

Some high schools offer early-college or dual-enrollment programs where students complete most coursework off-site; those students are still counted in the home high school's enrollment, even if they are rarely physically present on campus.

When comparing enrollment figures across countries in presentations, analysts often convert headcount to a per-1000-students basis; however this normalization is not applied inside the core database tables, which store only raw counts.

Cross-country enrollment benchmarks used in board materials (e.g., comparisons to Argentina, Chile, or OECD averages) are usually sourced from UNESCO or World Bank datasets and are not replicated in the local school data warehouse.

Several internal metrics, such as "instructional enrollment" or "funded enrollment", adjust the student count by removing students who were enrolled for less than a threshold number of days; these adjusted metrics do not appear in the base school tables.

Boundary changes (rezoning) can cause a school's enrollment to jump or drop sharply from one year to the next; these shifts are usually not flagged in the school table itself and must be inferred from district board resolutions or GIS boundary files.

Some specialized magnet programs operate as "schools within schools"; they may have their own reporting code for accountability purposes but share a single physical campus and enrollment count with the host school.

The district occasionally reuses campus codes for newly opened schools that replace closed ones on the same site; analysts doing longitudinal work should always join on both code and academic year to avoid conflating different institutions.

Alternative education centers (e.g., continuation, juvenile court schools) may report very small enrollments that fluctuate weekly; their codes sometimes appear or disappear in mid-year snapshots depending on contractual arrangements.

In older flat files, missing SAT section scores were sometimes encoded as 0 rather than left blank; analysts computing averages from those files need to verify whether zeros represent true scores or non-participation.

For internal dashboards, SAT scores are often converted from the old 2400-point scale to the newer 1600-point scale using concordance tables; these converted values may differ slightly from those reported directly by the testing agency.

The label "SAT participation rate" in summary tables may be computed either as test takers divided by October 12th-grade enrollment or by end-of-year graduates; the chosen denominator is rarely documented in the table schema.

Some school climate surveys ask students whether they "plan to take the SAT"; in a few presentations this intent measure has been accidentally labeled as an actual SAT participation metric, which can be misleading if reused without context.

Many small rural schools send their students to regional testing centers operated by neighboring districts; the testing center code, not the home school code, may appear in some raw SAT result files unless explicitly remapped.

The district's master data dictionary distinguishes between "operational" schools (currently serving students) and "administrative" schools (e.g., central office entities with codes but no enrolled students); only the former should appear in student-level SAT extracts.

Occasionally, schools may be temporarily closed due to construction or natural disasters and have their students reassigned; during these years, SAT scores might be attributed to the receiving school, but enrollment records may still reflect the original campus code.

A few independent charter schools report SAT and enrollment data on a fiscal-year basis aligned to their authorizer's calendar rather than the traditional academic year; their records are back-mapped into academic years in the warehouse using simple cutoff rules.

Teachers sometimes refer informally to the "average grade" of a school to mean the typical grade level represented (e.g., 5th grade for an elementary), which is unrelated to the effective grade metric used in analytics.

Some planning models use a synthetic "grade band" variable (ES, MS, HS, K-8, K-12, Other) derived from the underlying low and high grades; this band is for convenience only and is not intended for precise grade-level analysis.

In certain dashboards, schools with non-traditional grade spans (e.g., 4-9 academies) are forced into a single level (elementary, middle, or high) based on whichever span covers more grades; this simplification is not reflected in the raw tables.

When grade spans change (for example, an elementary adding grade 6), the change may be effective for scheduling but not immediately updated in state directories; for one or two years the reported high grade may lag the actual highest grade served.

Occasionally, schools pilot ungraded programs (e.g., ungraded primary) where students are not assigned a traditional grade level; in these cases, the low and high grade fields may carry placeholder values that do not map neatly onto standard grade-level analytics.
